challenges.title: Hello Rust
challenges.description: Print exactly `Hello, World!`. Expected output: `Hello, World!`.
challenges.hint: Use println! with exact text.

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: Read the requirement carefully and focus on producing the exact expected output or return value.

steps.title: Implement the core logic
steps.explanation: Apply the language feature that directly solves the task.

steps.title: Verify expected result
steps.explanation: Run the code with the example case and confirm the output/return value matches exactly.

challenges.hint: Multiply and pass result to println!.

challenges.hint: Three println! calls in exact order.

challenges.title: Area Pulse
challenges.description: Given `width = 4` and `height = 3`, print rectangle area. Expected output: `12`.
challenges.hint: Area is width * height.

steps.title: Understand the goal

challenges.title: Bool Blast
challenges.description:
Create two boolean variables: `let a = true;` and `let b = false;`. Print each on its own line. Expected output:
true
false
challenges.hint: Booleans print as lowercase true or false with println!.

steps.title: Declare boolean variables
steps.explanation: Use `let` to create two variables of type bool, one set to true and one to false.

steps.title: Print each boolean
steps.explanation: Use println! with a {} placeholder to display each boolean value on its own line.

steps.title: Verify expected result
steps.explanation: Run the code and confirm it prints true on the first line and false on the second line.

challenges.title: Half Split
challenges.description: Divide 7.0 by 2.0 using f64 variables and print the result. Expected output: `3.5`.
challenges.hint: Use f64 literals like 7.0 and 2.0 so Rust performs float division.

steps.title: Declare float variables
steps.explanation: Create two f64 variables holding 7.0 and 2.0 to ensure floating-point division.

steps.title: Divide and print
steps.explanation: Use the / operator inside println! to compute and display the result.

steps.title: Verify expected result
steps.explanation: Run the code and confirm the output is 3.5.

challenges.title: Length Check
challenges.description: Create `let word = "Rustacean";` and print its length. Expected output: `9`.
challenges.hint: String slices have a .len() method that returns the number of bytes.

steps.title: Store the string
steps.explanation: Use let to bind the string literal "Rustacean" to a variable.

steps.title: Print the length
steps.explanation: Call .len() on the string slice and pass the result to println!.

steps.title: Verify expected result
steps.explanation: Run the code and confirm it prints 9.

challenges.title: Even Gate
challenges.description: Create `let n = 4;`. Use if/else to check whether n is even or odd, then print the result. Expected output: `even`.
challenges.hint: Use the modulo operator % to check divisibility by 2.

steps.title: Declare the variable
steps.explanation: Create an integer variable n with value 4.

steps.title: Write the if/else check
steps.explanation: Use n % 2 == 0 as the condition. If true print "even", otherwise print "odd".

steps.title: Verify expected result
steps.explanation: Run the code and confirm it prints even.

challenges.title: Combo Print
challenges.description: Create `let city = "Tokyo";` and `let pop = 14;`. Print them in one line. Expected output: `Tokyo has 14 million people`.
challenges.hint: Use multiple {} placeholders in a single println! call.

steps.title: Declare variables
steps.explanation: Create a string slice for the city name and an integer for the population.

steps.title: Format the output
steps.explanation: Use println! with two {} placeholders to combine both values into one sentence.

steps.title: Verify expected result
steps.explanation: Run the code and confirm it prints Tokyo has 14 million people.

challenges.title: Sum Five
challenges.description: Use a for loop to sum the numbers from 1 to 5 (inclusive) and print the total. Expected output: `15`.
challenges.hint: Use `for i in 1..=5` for an inclusive range and accumulate into a mutable variable.

steps.title: Initialize a mutable accumulator
steps.explanation: Declare `let mut sum = 0;` so you can update the value inside the loop.

steps.title: Loop and accumulate
steps.explanation: Use `for i in 1..=5` to iterate from 1 to 5 inclusive, adding each value to sum.

steps.title: Verify expected result
steps.explanation: Run the code and confirm the output is 15.

challenges.title: Tuple Pop
challenges.description: Create `let pair = ("Rust", 2015);`. Unpack it into two variables and print them. Expected output: `Rust was released in 2015`.
challenges.hint: Use `let (name, year) = pair;` to destructure the tuple.

steps.title: Create the tuple
steps.explanation: Define a tuple containing a string slice and an integer.

steps.title: Destructure and print
steps.explanation: Use pattern matching with let to unpack the tuple into named variables, then print them.

steps.title: Verify expected result
steps.explanation: Run the code and confirm it prints Rust was released in 2015.

challenges.title: Char Count
challenges.description: Create `let text = "hello!";` and print the number of characters. Expected output: `6`.
challenges.hint: Use .chars().count() to count Unicode characters in a string.

steps.title: Store the string
steps.explanation: Bind the string literal to a variable with let.

steps.title: Count characters and print
steps.explanation: Call .chars() to get an iterator of characters, then .count() to get the total, and print it.

steps.title: Verify expected result
steps.explanation: Run the code and confirm it prints 6.

challenges.title: Type Shift
challenges.description: Create `let x: i32 = 10;`. Convert it to f64 and print it. Expected output: `10.5` after adding 0.5 to the converted value.
challenges.hint: Use the `as` keyword to convert between numeric types, e.g. `x as f64`.

steps.title: Declare the integer
steps.explanation: Create an i32 variable with value 10.

steps.title: Convert and compute
steps.explanation: Use `x as f64` to cast the integer to a float, then add 0.5 and store the result.

steps.title: Verify expected result
steps.explanation: Run the code and confirm the output is 10.5.

challenges.title: Mut Bump
challenges.description: Create `let mut count = 1;`. Increment it twice and print the final value. Expected output: `3`.
challenges.hint: Declare the variable with `mut` so you can reassign or modify it with +=.

steps.title: Declare a mutable variable
steps.explanation: Use `let mut` to create a variable whose value can be changed after creation.

steps.title: Increment and print
steps.explanation: Use += to add 1 twice, then print the result.

steps.title: Verify expected result
steps.explanation: Run the code and confirm the output is 3.

challenges.title: Glue Words
challenges.description: Use format! to join `let a = "Hello";` and `let b = "Rust";` into one string and print it. Expected output: `Hello, Rust!`.
challenges.hint: The format! macro works like println! but returns a String instead of printing.

steps.title: Declare the parts
steps.explanation: Create two string slice variables holding the words to join.

steps.title: Format and print
steps.explanation: Use format! to build a new String with placeholders, then print it with println!.

steps.title: Verify expected result
steps.explanation: Run the code and confirm it prints Hello, Rust!.

challenges.title: Min Pick
challenges.description: Create `let a = 8;` and `let b = 3;`. Use if/else to find and print the smaller value. Expected output: `3`.
challenges.hint: Compare a and b with < in an if expression and print the smaller one.

steps.title: Declare two variables
steps.explanation: Create two integer variables to compare.

steps.title: Compare and print the minimum
steps.explanation: Use an if/else to check which value is smaller and print it.

steps.title: Verify expected result
steps.explanation: Run the code and confirm the output is 3.

challenges.title: Odd Loop
challenges.description:
Use a for loop over 1..=10 and print only the odd numbers, each on its own line. Expected output:
1
3
5
7
9
challenges.hint: Inside the loop, use an if with the % operator to check for odd numbers.

steps.title: Set up the loop
steps.explanation: Use `for i in 1..=10` to iterate through numbers 1 to 10 inclusive.

steps.title: Filter odd numbers
steps.explanation: Inside the loop, check if i % 2 != 0 and only print when the condition is true.

steps.title: Verify expected result
steps.explanation: Run the code and confirm it prints 1, 3, 5, 7, 9 each on a separate line.

challenges.title: Match Day
challenges.description: Create `let day = 3;`. Use a match expression to print the weekday name where 1=Monday, 2=Tuesday, 3=Wednesday, and anything else prints "other". Expected output: `Wednesday`.
challenges.hint: Use `match day { 1 => ..., 2 => ..., _ => ... }` with a wildcard for the default case.

steps.title: Declare the variable
steps.explanation: Create an integer variable representing a day number.

steps.title: Write the match expression
steps.explanation: Use match with arms for 1, 2, 3 mapping to weekday names, and _ as the catch-all default.

steps.title: Verify expected result
steps.explanation: Run the code and confirm it prints Wednesday.

challenges.title: Rust Card
challenges.description:
Create variables `let name = "Ferris";`, `let age = 9;`, and `let is_crab = true;`. Print a three-line info card. Expected output:
Name: Ferris
Age: 9
Crab: true
challenges.hint: Use three separate println! calls, each with a {} placeholder for one variable.

steps.title: Declare all variables
steps.explanation: Create three variables of different types: a string slice, an integer, and a boolean.

steps.title: Print the info card
steps.explanation: Use three println! calls to display each variable with its label on a separate line.

steps.title: Verify expected result
steps.explanation: Run the code and confirm three lines appear: Name: Ferris, Age: 9, and Crab: true.
